merger_description:
Audiotonix Limited (Audiotonix) intends to acquire 100% of the shares and sole control of Soundtech Group S.r.l. (Soundtech Group) through 2 special purpose vehicles, Sound Bidco S.r.l and Sound Bidco (Denmark) ApS. Both special purpose vehicles will be 100% directly owned by A6 Audio Bidco Limited (A6 Audio), which, in turn, is 100% indirectly owned by Audiotonix. A6 Audio and Audiotonix are part of Audiotonix Group, a UK-headquartered group active in the design, engineering and manufacturing of professional audio mixing consoles, as well as associated equipment such as networking (allowing consoles to connect with other consoles), interface products (used to physically control music editing and mixing remotely from a console), software (e.g., audio processing, control software and operating systems) and spare parts.

Audiotonix is under the sole control of PAI Partners, a private equity firm with a focus on investing in business services, food and consumer, general industrials and healthcare.

In Australia, Audiotonix’s subsidiary Sound Devices is a wholesale supplier of wireless microphone systems.

Soundtech Group is a supplier in the professional audio industry which is active in the design, manufacturing and application of micro-electronic mechanisms.

Soundtech Group’s subsidiaries include DPA Microphones A/S (DPA Microphones), Austrian Audio GmbH (Austrian Audio) and Wisycom S.r.l. (Wisycom).

In Australia, DPA Microphones and Austrian Audio are wholesale suppliers of high-end microphones, and Wisycom is a wholesale supplier of wireless microphone systems and wireless in-ear monitoring systems.

events.title: Audiotonix - Soundtech Group - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by a division of the Commission constituted by a direction issued pursuant to section 19 of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Audiotonix Limited (Audiotonix)’s proposed acquisition, via Sound
Bidco S.r.l. and Sound Bidco (Denmark) ApS, of 100% of the shares
in Soundtech Group S.r.l. (Soundtech Group) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, Audiotonix, is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Audiotonix
Group Limited (Audiotonix Group), an UK-headquartered group
active in the design, engineering and manufacturing of professional
audio mixing consoles, associated equipment such as networking
(allowing consoles to connect with other consoles), interface
products (used to physically control music editing and mixing
remotely from a console), software (e.g., audio processing, control
software and operating systems) and spare parts. Audiotonix Group
is ultimately owned by PAI Partners S.à r.l. (PAI Partners), an
independent private equity firm headquartered in Paris, France. PAI
Partners invests in business services, food and consumer, general
industrials and healthcare.
In Australia, Audiotonix’s subsidiary Sound Devices LLC (Sound
Devices) is a wholesale supplier of portable and installed production
sound and video products for professional applications, including
wireless microphone systems.
The target, Soundtech Group, is a supplier of professional audio
micro-electronic mechanisms. Its subsidiaries include DPA
Microphones A/S (DPA Microphones), Austrian Audio GmbH
(Austrian Audio) and Wisycom S.r.l. (Wisycom).
In Australia, DPA Microphones and Austrian Audio are wholesale
suppliers of high-end microphones, and Wisycom is a wholesale
supplier of wireless microphone systems and wireless in-ear
monitoring systems.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
Audiotonix and Soundtech Group (together, the Parties) overlap in
the supply of wireless microphone systems in Australia. Soundtech
Group also supplies wireless in-ear monitoring systems in Australia.
Wireless microphone systems refer to audio solutions for use on
stage, on set or on the move that combine microphones, wireless
transmitters and receivers. Sound is recorded through a wireless
handheld microphone or through other types of microphones, such
as on-body or on-clothing microphones, that communicates with a
bodypack transmitter. The sound is transmitted to a wireless
receiver, which can be connected to various other
microphones/transmitters, and transfers the recorded sound to
other sound systems for processing. Antennas can be used as part
of the system to pick up radio frequency-transmitted sound over
longer distances.
Wireless in-ear monitoring systems refer to audio solutions used in
live, broadcast and theatrical environments and enable different
performers to hear all or relevant parts of the sound of the
performance. These systems combine wireless transmitters,
receivers worn on the body by the performers which transmit the
sound to an audio device, such as ear moulds, and combiners that
combine radio frequency outputs from several transmitters into a
single antenna.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• The Parties’ market share aggregation is estimated to be low on
any market definition, with an estimated post-acquisition market
share of below 10%.
• The Parties would continue to face competition from alternative
suppliers of wireless microphone and in-ear monitoring systems
in Australia, such as Sennheiser, Shure, Audio-Technica and
Lectrosonics.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.
